cue: One accumulator, but record its value after each step.
Carry a best-so-far and emit it once per element.
prompt: Implement `runningMax(a)` returning an array whose element `i` is the largest value among `a[0..i]`. `runningMax([])` is `[]`.

concept: count matches over a string domain (case-insensitive)
cue: Same tally loop, but the test is membership in a small set, either case.
prompt: Implement `countVowels(s)` returning how many characters of string `s` are vowels (`a`, `e`, `i`, `o`, `u`), upper or lower case.

concept: extreme by a derived key (nearest to target)
cue: Compare-and-keep, but rank by distance to the target, not by value.
prompt: Implement `closest(a, t)` returning the element of non-empty array `a` nearest to `t` (smallest `Math.abs(x - t)`); on ties keep the first.

cue: Walk one index across both arrays and tally where they agree.
Push the position when the test holds, not the element.
Track a best-so-far over each adjacent difference.
prompt: Implement `maxRise(a)` returning the largest increase between consecutive elements (`a[i] - a[i-1]`), or 0 if it never increases. Empty and single-element arrays give 0.

concept: reverse in place via two-pointer swap
cue: Two indices walking inward, swapping until they meet.
prompt: Implement `reverseInPlace(a)` that reverses array `a` in place and returns it. Do not build a new array or use `.reverse()` or spread.
note: In place uses O(1) extra space: swap the ends and walk inward, versus the spread copy which allocates a second array.

cue: One accumulator, but emit its value once per element.
prompt: Implement `runningSum(a)` returning an array whose element `i` is the sum of `a[0..i]`. `runningSum([])` is `[]`.

cue: One pass: consider what you must remember about earlier elements to score each one.
syntax: track the min seen so far
prompt: Implement `maxProfit(a)` returning the largest `a[j] - a[i]` for some `i < j`, or 0 if no later element is larger. Empty or single-element arrays give 0.
note: The trick is a single min-so-far carried left to right, so it stays O(n) (work grows in step with the array length) instead of comparing every pair, which is O(n^2).
mapsTo: LC 121 · Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

cue: Carry the best sum of a subarray ending at i: extend the previous one or restart at this element.
prompt: Implement `maxSubArray(a)` returning the largest sum of any non-empty contiguous subarray of `a`.
note: cur is the best sum ending at i: either start fresh at a[i] or extend the running one, whichever is larger. Seeding best and cur at a[0] is why the all-negative case still returns the least-negative element. One pass, O(n).

concept: for...in yields keys (index strings), not values
cue: for...in hands you keys; an array's keys are its indices, as strings.
note: for...in iterates keys, and array indices come out as strings ('0', '1', ...). For values use for...of; for real numeric indices use a counting loop.

concept: reduce with no initial value seeds from element 0
cue: With no second argument, reduce starts from element 0 and folds the rest into it.
note: With no initial value, reduce uses element 0 as the seed and never calls the function here. On an empty array with no init it throws instead, which is why passing an init like 0 is the safe habit.
